votes.reason: The Python code accurately implements the Coq specification, checking the same conditions for res == -1 and 1 <= res <= n - 1, including the array element comparisons and bounds.
The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification, checking the same array conditions for res == -1 and 1 <= res <= n - 1.
The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification, checking the same conditions for res == -1 and 1 <= res <= n - 1, including the array bounds and comparisons.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq spec. When res=-1, it checks all consecutive pairs are non-decreasing (arr[i] >= arr[i-1]). When 1 <= res <= n-1, it checks arr[res] < arr[res-1] and all pairs after res are non-decreasing. The Coq uses 1-based indexing for the position variable but nth_error with Z.to_nat converts to 0-based array access, matching Python's direct 0-based indexing. The conditions ai >= ap (non-decreasing) and ai < ap (decreasing) are correctly translated.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq spec: returns True when res=-1 and array is non-decreasing, returns True when 1<=res<=n-1 with arr[res]<arr[res-1] and all subsequent pairs are non-decreasing, and returns False otherwise. The index handling matches (Coq uses 1-based indices in the spec while Python uses 0-based, but the translation correctly maps res to the same array positions).

The Python code correctly implements the Coq spec: returns True when res=-1 and array is non-decreasing, returns True when 1<=res<=n-1 and arr[res]<arr[res-1] (a decrease) with all subsequent pairs non-decreasing, and returns False otherwise. The Coq spec uses 1-indexed positions with Z.of_nat(length arr)-1 as upper bound, which corresponds to Python's 0-indexed range(1,n) where n=len(arr). The conditions match: Coq's ai>=ap means non-decreasing (Python checks arr[i]<arr[i-1] returns False), and Coq's ai<ap at res means a decrease exists there (Python checks arr[res]>=arr[res-1] returns False if no decrease).
Both require: res=-1 iff the array is nondecreasing; otherwise res in [1,n-1] with a strict drop at res and nondecreasing from res+1 onward. Index bounds and comparisons align exactly.
Matches Coq: res = -1 checks nondecreasing across entire array; otherwise requires a drop at res and nondecreasing for all j > res up to end. Index bounds and edge cases align.

Python mirrors the two Coq cases: res = -1 enforces nondecreasing across all adjacent pairs; otherwise 1 <= res <= n-1 requires a strict drop at res and nondecreasing thereafter. Index ranges and comparisons align with nth_error bounds, and invalid res values correctly yield False.
